name: Runge Kutta method composition inverse
statement_text:
Let m denote a Runge–Kutta method. Then

                           [m · m−1 ] = [m−1 · m] = 1.
statement_latex:
\begin{theorem}
Let $m$ denote a Runge--Kutta method. Then
\[
[m \cdot m^{-1}] = [m^{-1} \cdot m] = 1.
\]
proof_text:
Proof. The tableaux for the two composite methods m · m−1 and m−1 · m
are, respectively,

       c1    a11   a12   ···   a1s        0          0        ···      0
       c2    a21   a22   ···   a2s        0          0        ···      0
        ..    ..    ..          ..        ..         ..                ..
         .     .     .           .         .          .                 .
       cs    as1   as2   ···   ass        0          0        ···      0

       c1    b1    b2    ···   bs     a11 − b1   a12 − b2     ···   a1s − bs
       c2    b1    b2    ···   bs     a21 − b1   a22 − b2     ···   a2s − bs
        ..    ..    ..          ..        ..         ..                 ..
         .     .     .           .         .          .                  .
       cs    b1    b2    ···   bs     as1 − b1   as2 − b2     ···   ass − bs
             b1    b2    ···   bs       −b1        −b2        ···     −bs

and
     "
 c1 − sj=1 bj     a11 − b1    a12 − b2    ···    a1s − bs       0     0    ···     0
     "
 c2 − sj=1 bj     a21 − b1    a22 − b2    ···    a2s − bs       0     0    ···     0
      ..              ..          ..                 ..         ..    ..           ..
       .               .           .                  .          .     .            .
     "s
 cs − j=1 bj      as1 − b1    as2 − b2    ···    ass − bs       0     0    ···     0
     "
 c1 − sj=1 bj        −b1         −b2      ···      −bs         a11   a12   ···   a1s
     "
 c2 − sj=1 bj        −b1         −b2      ···      −bs         a21   a22   ···   a2s
      ..              ..          ..                ..          ..    ..          ..
       .               .           .                 .           .     .           .
     "s
 cs − j=1 bj         −b1         −b2      ···      −bs         as1   as2   ···   ass      .
                     −b1         −b2      ···      −bs         b1    b2    ···   bs

Each of these methods is P -reducible to the methods m and m−1 , respectively,
but in each case with b replaced by the zero vector, so that each lies in the
equivalence class 1.
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
Let $m$ denote a Runge--Kutta method with tableau $(A,b,c)$ and $s$ stages.
Let $m^{-1}$ be the inverse method constructed from $m$.
Let $[\,\cdot\,]$ denote the equivalence class under the relation that two methods produce identical numerical results for sufficiently small $h$ for Lipschitz problems.
Let $\mathbf{1}$ be the identity equivalence class containing methods that map an initial value to itself for sufficiently small $h$.

variables.name: m
variables.definition: a Runge–Kutta method, typically represented as a tableau (A, b, c) with s stages

variables.name: m^{-1}
variables.definition: the inverse Runge–Kutta method constructed from m via a specific tableau transformation that undoes the work of m

equivalence class of Runge–Kutta methods under the equivalence relation defined earlier in the text (e.g., methods that produce identical numerical results for sufficiently small step sizes h for Lipschitz continuous problems)
the identity equivalence class, containing any method that maps an initial value to an equal value for sufficiently small h

preamble: The text defines an algebraic structure on equivalence classes of Runge–Kutta methods, including a multiplicative operation (·), an identity class 1, and an inverse method m^{-1} constructed from m. The theorem states that the equivalence class of the product of a method and its inverse equals the identity class.
